text: She was offered a redo right mini thoracotomy approach for mitral valve replacement.
The left atrium was open
in the interatrial groove.
The left atrial retractor was inserted and the left atrium exposed.
Visualizing the valve was easy.
One could see that the valve was clearly well seated.

Anuloplasty ring removal was performed by using a 15 blade knife in a long handle to take down all the pannus surrounding or covering the ring, making sure that we would extract every single stitch or anuloplasty suture.
This was a tedious process that required a combination of 15 blade and Metzenbaum scissors as well as a plaque elevator.
The transcatheter valve dissection was performed using a 15 blade knife making sure that we
removed all the adhesions of the native leaflets of the mitral valve to the cage of the S3 valve.
This process was also tedious.
Visualization was very good at the 12 o'clock, 9 o'clock, and 3 o'clock positions.
But at the 6 o'clock position, visualization was poor given the presence of the S3 proximal aspect of the cage.
With this in mind, I used electrocautery with low energy at 10 to be able to dissect away the posterior leaflet, native leaflets, to the cage, effectively separating it completely.
All the native cords were resected, attaching it to the posterior medial muscles to be able to remove the cage and the ring
Care was taken not to injure the annulus and not to create an AV groove separation during the dissection.

Once all the attachments of the valve to the native leaflets and especially to the posterior medial papillary muscle were taken down using a combination of bovie cautery, a 10 and 15 blade knife, I was able to completely detach the valve from the cardiac structures.
Again, making sure that there was no injury to that posterior annulus.
The valve was removed and sized to a 25 mechanical valve.
One could see that the lateral lateral papillary muscle continued to be attached, but the posterior medial had no cords.
The valve sutures were placed.
These were non-plagidated 2-O ethebond.
care was taken to capture the entirety of the scar tissue located at the annulus.
The posterior medial papillary muscle was re-anchored to the annulus using a Gore-Tex suture and the mechanical valve was seated in place.
The left atrium was closed in one layer and this shows the appearance of the valve after explantation.
One can see the pannus growth
